title: A resolution recognizing the contributions of the Charles B. Rangel Graduate Fellowship Program, the Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Graduate Fellowship Program, the William D. Clarke, Sr. Diplomatic Security Fellowship, and the Donald M. Payne International Development Graduate Fellowship Program in advancing the national security and the development and diplomacy efforts of the United States.
plain_summary: This resolution is about four special programs that help train people from different backgrounds to work for the US government in other countries. These programs help people become diplomats and work on international development projects around the world. The Senate thinks these programs are important because they make sure America has skilled workers from all kinds of communities representing the country overseas. The resolution says these programs help keep America safe and should be protected because they are required by law.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. (text: CR S1667-1668)

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes the contributions of four fellowship programs that help train people for careers in American diplomacy and international development. These programs are the Charles B. Rangel Graduate Fellowship Program, the Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Graduate Fellowship Program, the William D. Clarke Sr. Diplomatic Security Fellowship, and the Donald M. Payne International Development Graduate Fellowship Program. These programs help advance America's national security and efforts in diplomacy and development around the world.

sections.title: Background and Reasons
sections.text: The Department of State, the United States Agency for International Development (a government agency that provides aid to other countries), and other foreign affairs agencies need workers with many different talents, skills, and experiences. These workers help protect American citizens living in other countries, create business opportunities for American companies overseas, and carry out American foreign policy. Congress has required by law that the Department of State and the aid agency hire employees based on merit who reflect the diverse backgrounds of the American people they represent abroad. In nineteen ninety, Congress changed a law to allow the Secretary of State to give grants to colleges or students to increase knowledge about and interest in working for the Foreign Service. This law specifically focused on minority students to make sure recruitment efforts were fair and drew on the strength of all American citizens. Because of this law, the Department of State started the Thomas R. Pickering Foreign Affairs Fellowship in nineteen ninety-two, the Charles B. Rangel International Affairs Program in two thousand two, and the William D. Clarke Sr. Diplomatic Security Fellowship in two thousand twenty-three. These programs help include more students who receive Pell grants (financial aid for low-income students) and first-generation college graduates in the Foreign Service. Most current fellows have received Pell grants. The Charles B. Rangel and Thomas R. Pickering programs are the Department of State's main programs for recruiting top talent. They are based on merit and financial need and are highly competitive, with less than five percent of applicants accepted each year. All fellows must pass the same tough selection, hiring, and security clearance process as all other Foreign Service members. Research shows that having a workforce that represents all of America helps create better national security outcomes. This happens because diverse workers provide a wider range of perspectives, experiences, and cultural understanding. This leads to better threat identification, more creative solutions, and stronger diplomatic relationships around the world. Fellowship programs that promote hiring candidates from historically excluded groups and those with financial needs represent smart investments that are vital for building a strong, merit-based, capable, and diverse national security workforce. Congress has authorized each of these fellowship programs with support from both political parties. Congress recognizes how important these programs are for expanding merit-based and need-based recruitment from a wide geographic and economically diverse talent pool. This includes people from all fifty states and more than five hundred colleges and universities. Historically Black Colleges and Universities, Hispanic-serving institutions, other minority-serving institutions, and other colleges including community colleges and trade schools serve populations historically excluded from the Department of State and the aid agency. These schools prepare the next generation of international affairs professionals with the core skills necessary to meet America's global diplomatic and development needs. The Secretary of State and the Administrator of the United States Agency for International Development are required by law to talk with Congress before taking steps to change these programs.

sections.title: Senate Resolution
sections.text: The Senate recognizes the importance of efforts to recruit, hire, and keep employees from the broadest talent pool for American foreign affairs agencies. This helps the United States stay globally competitive and ensures that America's diplomatic and development agencies remain the best in the world. The Senate reaffirms that the four fellowship programs are required by law and were created with support from both political parties. These programs address recognized problems that have affected the Department of State and the aid agency for decades, including the exclusion of women, racial and ethnic minority groups, and economically disadvantaged and rural populations. The Senate emphasizes how important international affairs fellowships and similar career entry programs are to American national security and foreign policy. The Senate recognizes the substantial investment by American taxpayers in ensuring the Department of State and the aid agency can recruit top talent from across the country, provide them with important training, and strengthen America's development and diplomatic abilities. These efforts are hurt by attempts to dismantle these programs, which wastes taxpayer money and weakens national security.
